ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002572 covers biryani as a category. No specific code exists for vegetarian mock mutton biryani; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers biryani as a spiced rice dish. No specific code for vegetarian mock mutton variant; closest_parent applied.

preparation_method: dum cooking (slow steam cooking in sealed pot)
layering of rice and mock mutton
pressure sealing with dough or foil
main_ingredients: mock mutton (soya-based or textured vegetable protein)
whole spices (cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, bay leaf, star anise)
ground spices (biryani masala, turmeric, chilli powder, coriander)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of mock mutton dum biryani is approximately 400 g, comprising a generous mound of spiced basmati rice layered with mock mutton pieces, fried onions, and garnished with mint and coriander, typically served with raita or curry.

nutrient_source.title: Biryani (vegetarian, soya-based) — estimated from ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 (Vegetable Biryani and Soya Chunk entries) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Basmati rice cooked, Textured Soy Protein)
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition) and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for mock mutton dum biryani. Values estimated by combining ICMR-NIN 2017 data for vegetable biryani (cooked basmati rice with spices and vegetables) and soya chunk / textured vegetable protein entries from ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy. Mock mutton is modelled as hydrated textured soy protein (TVP). Proportions assumed: ~60% cooked basmati rice, ~20% hydrated TVP mock mutton, ~10% onion/tomato/spice mix, ~10% oil/ghee. Weighted composite per 100 g of finished dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Basmati rice (cooked) and mixed rice dishes; Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mock mutton dum biryani. GI estimated at 56 based on cooked basmati rice GI of 50–58 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021) as the dominant carbohydrate component (~60% of dish weight). The addition of soya protein, fat from ghee/oil, and dietary fibre from vegetables and TVP is expected to modestly lower the effective GI relative to plain basmati rice; 56 is used as a conservative mid-range estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (56 × 90.0) / 100 = 50.4, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 22.5 × 4 = 90.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~1240 mg per restaurant serving of 400 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 200–250 g and request reduced salt and ghee preparation, as a full restaurant serving can contribute approximately 50–60% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~56) and high glycemic load per restaurant serving (~50.4 g GL)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a reduced portion (150–200 g) of this biryani, pair it with a non-starchy vegetable side or raita to blunt the postprandial glucose response, and monitor blood glucose given the substantial carbohydrate load from basmati rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ghee (~1.9 g per 100 g) and moderate sodium; soya protein component is cardioprotective
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may benefit from the soya protein content (associated with LDL reduction) but should request preparation with minimal ghee and be mindful of the sodium load, keeping portions to 200–250 g to limit saturated fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mock Mutton Dum Biryani (Vegetarian) is a plant-based adaptation of the classic South Asian dum biryani, in which traditional mutton is replaced by mock mutton — typically textured soy protein (TVP) or soya chunks — that has been marinated in yogurt and spices to mimic the texture and flavour of slow-cooked lamb. The dish is prepared using the dum method: layers of parboiled basmati rice and spiced mock mutton are sealed in a heavy-bottomed pot or handi with dough or foil and slow-cooked over low heat, allowing steam to circulate and infuse the rice with aromatic spices including cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, star anise, saffron, and biryani masala. The result is a fragrant, layered rice dish with a slightly chewy mock meat component, caramelised fried onions (birista), and fresh mint and coriander. In Singapore, this dish is served at Indian vegetarian restaurants such as Jewel of India and is popular among vegetarians, vegans (when ghee is substituted), and those observing Hindu dietary practices. Nutritionally, it provides moderate energy (~168 kcal per 100 g), meaningful plant protein from soya (~7.2 g per 100 g), and dietary fibre (~2.1 g per 100 g). The medium glycemic index (~56) and high glycemic load per restaurant serving make portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is moderate to high in restaurant preparations, warranting caution for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The soya protein component may confer a modest cardioprotective benefit through LDL cholesterol reduction.
common_user_queries: What is mock mutton made of in biryani?
Is vegetarian biryani good for diabetics?
Difference between mock mutton and real mutton biryani
Is dum biryani high in sodium?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for mock mutton dum biryani as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN 2017 vegetable biryani and soya chunk entries combined with USDA SR Legacy data for cooked basmati rice and textured soy protein, using assumed proportions. GI is analogously estimated from basmati rice GI data in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level sources are high-quality approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates; a direct laboratory analysis of this specific restaurant dish would be required for high confidence. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 because this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived B12 sources; confirmed zero by formulation (soya TVP and plant ingredients contain no B12). 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed zero as no partially hydrogenated oils are used in traditional dum biryani preparation. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN 2017 for this food category and is not expected to be significant in a plant-based rice dish. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN 2017 for this food category. 6. GI set to 56 as an analogous estimate based on cooked basmati rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2021); no direct GI measurement exists for this dish. 7. Serving size of 400 g is a typical Indian restaurant portion for a biryani main course. 8. Halal status assumed as the restaurant (Jewel of India) is an Indian vegetarian establishment; no pork or alcohol ingredients are used. 9. Vegan status is false because ghee (clarified butter) and dairy yogurt are typically used; a vegan variant is possible on request. 10. FoodOn URI FOODON_00002572 and FoodEx2 code A0FKP are used as closest_parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for vegetarian mock mutton biryani.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected food_name spelling from 'Briyani' (colloquial) to 'Biryani' (canonical English) while retaining 'Briyani' as an alias.
Added all mandatory Singaporean-dish local names: Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi.
